question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Short answer / summary - Most likely root cause: a mismatch between the SharePoint ACL (folder permissions) and the Sensitivity Label’s encryption group plus token caching. The folder had Unique Permissions and was encrypted with a sensitivity label that referenced a different Azure AD group than the SharePoint ACL; users with cached tokens could access temporarily until tokens refreshed, causing the random/partial behaviour (including the “works in Incognito” symptom)
context: SOURCE 31: denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection Notable: When applying the Sensitivity Label, the option “Preserve custom permissions” was not activated. Result: • Existing SharePoint permissions formally remained • Encryption, however, applies only to explicitly defined groups This creates a situation where: SharePoint says → Access allowed AIP encryption says → Access only with key permission Depending on token refresh timing, access is granted or blocked. 24.03.25 14:35 Reproduction in test tenant: • Folder with Unique Permissions created • Sensitivity Label applied afterward • Different users tested Result: Users with older access tokens could temporarily access. After token renewal, access blocked. 25.03.25 09:15 Root cause defined: The folder was: 1. Renamed 2. Assigned Unique Permissions 3. Subsequently encrypted with a Sensitivity Label The encryption defined a different permission group than the SharePoint ACL. Token caching caused inconsistent behavior. 25.03.25 11:40 – Actions • Sensitivity Label removed • Folder reassigned with correct group reference • Label reapplied with correct group integration • “Compliance_Core” and “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” structurally aligned • Tests performed with multiple user accounts • Tokens intentionally invalidated 26.03.25 – Monitoring Multiple users tested Access consistently possible No further error messages 28.03.25 15:42 – Customer feedback Audit team confirms stable access for all employees. Ticket closure 28.03.25 16:02 Dear Contact Person 02 The root cause was an inconsistent combination of Unique SharePoint permissions and a subsequently applied Sensitivity Label encryption. Due to differing group references between ACL and encryption, inconsistent access behavior occurred, amplified by token caching. The permission structure was aligned and tested. Access is now stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 30: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF25: SharePoint – Project folder “Compliance_Review” partially not visible Ticket number: TI-1578891 Company: Customer Company 25 AG Contact person: Contact Person 02 Owner: Employee 27 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 18.03.25 08:43 Activities 18.03.25 08:36 Subject: Access to Compliance_Review folder missing for some users From: Contact Person 02 Good morning As part of our internal audit, we have identified that some employees do not have access to the folder /Projects/Compliance_Review_2025 Others can see it without issue. Please review. Kind regards Contact Person 02 18.03.25 08:43 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 27 Status: In Progress Notes 18.03.25 09:15 Helpdesk asks: • Which users exactly are affected? • Since when? • Screenshot of error message? • Access via browser or Teams? 18.03.25 10:02 Customer responds: Affected users: • M. Keller • S. Braun • T. Widmer Error message: Item not found Access via browser (Edge). Noticed since last week, but unclear exactly since when. 18.03.25 10:25 Helpdesk asks further: • Are users members of the project group? • Were permissions recently changed? • Does it affect only this folder or others as well? 18.03.25 11:18 Customer: We restructured several project groups last week. New groups were created: • COMPLIANCE_TEAM • COMPLIANCE_EXT Folder may have been newly inherited. Other folders work. 18.03.25 13:05 Helpdesk checks SharePoint permissions: • Folder has Unique Permissions • Permission via group “SP_Compliance_Main” • This group contains “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” Test with working user → access OK Test with affected user → no access 19.03.25 09:12 Helpdesk asks: Are the users possibly assigned via multiple groups? Were there recent role changes (HR)? 19.03.25 10:40 Customer responds: Two users changed departments internally (Finance → Risk). One works 50% in both teams. Could that be relevant? 19.03.25 11:05 Helpdesk checks group memberships: All three users are members of “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”. No obvious differences. 19.03.25 14:55 Customer reports: One user had access briefly today, then not again. After restart it did not work. 20.03.25 08:20 Helpdesk asks: Are users accessing from different devices? Company laptop or private device? With or without VPN? 20.03.25 09:03 Customer: Two via company laptop One via private device VPN not required (cloud-only environment). 20.03.25 10:45 Helpdesk checks: • Clear browser cache • Test in Incognito mode • Test in another browser Result: Incognito → access partially possible 20.03.25 15:10 Customer: After signing out of all Office apps, it worked briefly. Later not visible again. 21.03.25 08:55 Helpdesk asks: Was the folder possibly moved or renamed? Was the Sensitivity Label changed? Is the document library fully loaded? 21.03.25 10:18 Customer: Folder was renamed 2 weeks ago from “Compliance_Audit” to “Compliance_Review_2025”. Sensitivity Label “Confidential – Internal” applied. 21.03.25 13:30 Helpdesk checks: Label requires: • Encryption • Access only for group “Compliance_Core” Notable: “Compliance_Core” is not identical to “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”. However, according to SharePoint, permission is still managed via “SP_Compliance_Main”. Unclear correlation. 21.03.25 16:10 Ticket remains open due to inconsistent behavior. Status: In Progress 22.03.25 09:05 Internal note – Employee 27 Since the behavior remains inconsistent (partial access, partial denial, partial access only in Incognito mode), the ticket is escalated for in-depth analysis. Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – M365 / Identity 22.03.25 09:12 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 11 Status: In Progress 22.03.25 10:25 Analysis by System Engineer 11 Step 1 – Sensitivity Label review Label “Confidential – Internal” checked: • Encryption active • Access only for members of Azure AD group “Compliance_Core” • Automatic inheritance for folders enabled Notable: Folder “Compliance_Review_2025” has Unique Permissions. Label was applied AFTER renaming. Hypothesis: Conflict between SharePoint permissions and AIP label encryption. 22.03.25 11:40 Test: • User added directly to “Compliance_Core” → Access immediately possible After removal from “Compliance_Core” → Access denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - What to fix now: align the encryption group and the SharePoint permission group (or add required users to the label-encryption group), remove and reapply the label correctly, then invalidate user tokens so claims are refreshed. These exact remediation steps were applied successfully in the incident and restored stable access
context: SOURCE 31: denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - Because the label was applied after the unique permissions and “Preserve custom permissions” was not enabled, SharePoint’s ACL still showed access allowed while the encryption policy required a different group membership. Depending on whether a user’s authentication token still carried the older claims, access could be granted or blocked, causing inconsistent results (including cases where Incognito worked temporarily)

answer: . - Force token refresh for affected users (have them sign out of Office apps, browsers, and/or revoke refresh tokens via Azure AD) to eliminate cached claims and validate access consistently
context: SOURCE 31: denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection Notable: When applying the Sensitivity Label, the option “Preserve custom permissions” was not activated. Result: • Existing SharePoint permissions formally remained • Encryption, however, applies only to explicitly defined groups This creates a situation where: SharePoint says → Access allowed AIP encryption says → Access only with key permission Depending on token refresh timing, access is granted or blocked. 24.03.25 14:35 Reproduction in test tenant: • Folder with Unique Permissions created • Sensitivity Label applied afterward • Different users tested Result: Users with older access tokens could temporarily access. After token renewal, access blocked. 25.03.25 09:15 Root cause defined: The folder was: 1. Renamed 2. Assigned Unique Permissions 3. Subsequently encrypted with a Sensitivity Label The encryption defined a different permission group than the SharePoint ACL. Token caching caused inconsistent behavior. 25.03.25 11:40 – Actions • Sensitivity Label removed • Folder reassigned with correct group reference • Label reapplied with correct group integration • “Compliance_Core” and “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” structurally aligned • Tests performed with multiple user accounts • Tokens intentionally invalidated 26.03.25 – Monitoring Multiple users tested Access consistently possible No further error messages 28.03.25 15:42 – Customer feedback Audit team confirms stable access for all employees. Ticket closure 28.03.25 16:02 Dear Contact Person 02 The root cause was an inconsistent combination of Unique SharePoint permissions and a subsequently applied Sensitivity Label encryption. Due to differing group references between ACL and encryption, inconsistent access behavior occurred, amplified by token caching. The permission structure was aligned and tested. Access is now stable. Kind regards Contact Person 02 18.03.25 08:43 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 27 Status: In Progress Notes 18.03.25 09:15 Helpdesk asks: • Which users exactly are affected? • Since when? • Screenshot of error message? • Access via browser or Teams? 18.03.25 10:02 Customer responds: Affected users: • M. Keller • S. Braun • T. Widmer Error message: Item not found Access via browser (Edge). Noticed since last week, but unclear exactly since when. 18.03.25 10:25 Helpdesk asks further: • Are users members of the project group? • Were permissions recently changed? • Does it affect only this folder or others as well? 18.03.25 11:18 Customer: We restructured several project groups last week. New groups were created: • COMPLIANCE_TEAM • COMPLIANCE_EXT Folder may have been newly inherited. Other folders work. 18.03.25 13:05 Helpdesk checks SharePoint permissions: • Folder has Unique Permissions • Permission via group “SP_Compliance_Main” • This group contains “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” Test with working user → access OK Test with affected user → no access 19.03.25 09:12 Helpdesk asks: Are the users possibly assigned via multiple groups? Were there recent role changes (HR)? 19.03.25 10:40 Customer responds: Two users changed departments internally (Finance → Risk). One works 50% in both teams. Could that be relevant? 19.03.25 11:05 Helpdesk checks group memberships: All three users are members of “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”. No obvious differences. 19.03.25 14:55 Customer reports: One user had access briefly today, then not again. After restart it did not work. 20.03.25 08:20 Helpdesk asks: Are users accessing from different devices? Company laptop or private device? With or without VPN? 20.03.25 09:03 Customer: Two via company laptop One via private device VPN not required (cloud-only environment). 20.03.25 10:45 Helpdesk checks: • Clear browser cache • Test in Incognito mode • Test in another browser Result: Incognito → access partially possible 20.03.25 15:10 Customer: After signing out of all Office apps, it worked briefly. Later not visible again. 21.03.25 08:55 Helpdesk asks: Was the folder possibly moved or renamed? Was the Sensitivity Label changed? Is the document library fully loaded? 21.03.25 10:18 Customer: Folder was renamed 2 weeks ago from “Compliance_Audit” to “Compliance_Review_2025”. Sensitivity Label “Confidential – Internal” applied. 21.03.25 13:30 Helpdesk checks: Label requires: • Encryption • Access only for group “Compliance_Core” Notable: “Compliance_Core” is not identical to “COMPLIANCE_TEAM”. However, according to SharePoint, permission is still managed via “SP_Compliance_Main”. Unclear correlation. 21.03.25 16:10 Ticket remains open due to inconsistent behavior. Status: In Progress 22.03.25 09:05 Internal note – Employee 27 Since the behavior remains inconsistent (partial access, partial denial, partial access only in Incognito mode), the ticket is escalated for in-depth analysis. Assigned Group changed to: BU Infrastructure – M365 / Identity 22.03.25 09:12 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 11 Status: In Progress 22.03.25 10:25 Analysis by System Engineer 11 Step 1 – Sensitivity Label review Label “Confidential – Internal” checked: • Encryption active • Access only for members of Azure AD group “Compliance_Core” • Automatic inheritance for folders enabled Notable: Folder “Compliance_Review_2025” has Unique Permissions. Label was applied AFTER renaming. Hypothesis: Conflict between SharePoint permissions and AIP label encryption. 22.03.25 11:40 Test: • User added directly to “Compliance_Core” → Access immediately possible After removal from “Compliance_Core” → Access denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - Monitor sign-in and access logs to confirm tokens and group claims are correct. Preventive and governance actions (to avoid recurrence) - Align the SharePoint permission model and labeling practice with your documented architecture: use dedicated SharePoint resource groups (not functional groups) nested consistently, avoid parallel groups with the same name, and document changes before setting Unique Permissions or applying labels
context: SOURCE 24: ﻿ general info & description SharePoint Permission & Group Architecture – Customer Company 25 Version: 1.0 Date: 20.06.2025 Author: M365 / Identity Engineering Notes: Initial creation based on project structure IT-Projects This document describes the architectural and governance specifications for permission structures in SharePoint Online at Customer Company 25 AG. The objective is a consistent, maintainable, and auditable permission structure for project folders within SharePoint document libraries. Core principles: • No direct user permissions at folder or library level • Permissions exclusively via dedicated Azure AD groups • Clear separation between functional groups and SharePoint resource groups • Documented group nesting • No parallel or historical groups with identical purpose Group model A distinction is made between two group types: 1. Functional groups Example: IT-Projects Finance-Team Risk-Team 2. SharePoint resource groups Example: SP_Project_IT SP_Project_IT_Lead Functional groups must never receive permissions directly in SharePoint. They are nested into dedicated SharePoint groups. Group lifecycle • New project groups must be uniquely named • Historical groups (e.g., *_v1) must be structurally removed before deactivation • No parallel existence of old and new resource groups • Group changes must be documented MUST BE OBSERVED: SharePoint evaluates effective permissions based on the current group nesting in Entra ID. Incorrectly nested groups result in missing effective permissions. design Architecture overview User → Member of functional group → Functional group member in SharePoint resource group → SharePoint resource group has folder permission Permission model Folder or library permissions are assigned exclusively to SharePoint resource groups. Example: Folder: /Projects/2025_Migration Authorized: SP_Project_IT Structure: IT-Projects └── SP_Project_IT SP_Project_IT_Lead └── SP_Project_IT Unique Permissions Unique Permissions may only be set if: • It is documented which resource group is referenced • No old or deactivated group is still nested • The target group is clearly active When creating a new resource group, it must be verified: • Whether the old version is still referenced • Whether the nesting has been updated • Whether users are authorized only indirectly and not directly Synchronization & Propagation After changes to groups: • Wait for synchronization time • Verify effective permissions • Do not allow parallel groups with identical scope decision tree & use cases 1. Question: Is SharePoint Online generally accessible? No → The issue is not related to the permission model. Yes → Proceed to question 2. 2. Question: Does access work for other users? No → Check folder permissions. Yes → Proceed to question 3. 3. Question: Is the affected user directly a member of the resource group? Yes → The issue is not related to nesting. No → Proceed to question 4. 4. Question: Is the functional group correctly nested in the resource group? No → Review group structure. Yes → Proceed to question 5. 5. Question: Do old or deactivated groups with similar names exist? Yes → Consolidate group structure. No → Further analysis required.

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . - When applying a Sensitivity Label that enforces encryption, verify whether “Preserve custom permissions” or equivalent is needed and ensure the label’s encryption groups are identical to the SharePoint resource group object IDs (not just names) — SharePoint evaluates by object ID
